name: Moneva, Oliete, and Muniesa Loop
name_original: Moneva, Oliete y Muniesa - circular por la estepa toledana
description: This moderate 41.6-mile (67.0 km) road cycling route through Moneva, Oliete, and Muniesa offers dry heaths and reservoir views.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Blesa offer a diverse landscape within the Aragon region of Spain, characterized by varied topography and significant elevation changes. Cyclists can find routes ranging from moderate valley paths to challenging ascents through mountainous areas. The region is known for its unique historical mining landscapes, which provide a distinctive backdrop for rides. This blend of natural beauty and cultural heritage makes Blesa an appealing destination for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Blesa The most popular road cycling route is Moneva, Oliete, and Muniesa Loop, a 41.6 miles (66.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 8 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers a balanced ride through the local terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Sierra de Cucalón y Chevrons de Oriche - return from Huesa del Común, a difficult 66.1 miles (106.4 km) path. This route features substantial climbs and descents, providing panoramic views of the surrounding area. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto de Rudilla – Puerto de Fonfría (1470 m) loop from Estación de Plou, a 79.5 miles (128.0 km) trail leading through mountain passes, often completed in about 6 hours 16 minutes. Road cycling around Blesa is defined by varied topography, historical mining landscapes, and significant elevation changes. The network offers options for moderate and difficult ability levels, catering to different cycling preferences. The routes in Blesa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 60 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Blesa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Blesa?
A: There are over a dozen road cycling routes around Blesa, offering a mix of challenges. You'll find 4 moderate routes and 9 difficult routes, catering to various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Blesa?
A: Road cycling around Blesa offers a diverse topography, characteristic of the Aragon region. You can expect varied landscapes with significant elevation changes, ranging from valley paths to more demanding ascents through mountainous areas. The region also features unique historical mining landscapes, providing a distinctive backdrop for your rides.

Q: Are there challenging routes for experienced road cyclists in Blesa?
A: Yes, Blesa offers several challenging routes for experienced road cyclists. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31519965" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Rudilla – Puerto de Fonfría (1470 m) loop from Estación de Plou&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 128 km trail with over 1900 meters of elevation gain, leading through mountain passes.

Q: Are there any moderate or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in Blesa?
A: While Blesa is known for its challenging terrain, there are moderate options available. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970303275" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moneva, Oliete, and Muniesa Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate route, covering 66.9 km with an elevation gain of around 880 meters, offering a balanced ride through the local landscape.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or points of interest I can see along the road cycling routes?
A: As you cycle around Blesa, you can encounter several interesting points. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5199793" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Rudilla&lt;/a&gt; is a significant mountain pass, and you might pass through settlements like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/8027164" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monforte de Moyuela&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5224874" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Segura de los Baños&lt;/a&gt;. The region's historical mining landscapes also offer an 'open-air museum' experience with informational panels.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Blesa?
A: Yes, many of the routes around Blesa are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970303275" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moneva, Oliete, and Muniesa Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6930053" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monforte de Moyuela – Muniesa loop from Monforte de Moyuela&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Blesa?
A: Given the varied topography and potential for significant elevation changes, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in Blesa. Temperatures are milder, and the landscapes are particularly vibrant, making for a more enjoyable experience than the heat of summer or colder winter months.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Blesa?
A: The road cycling routes around Blesa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the unique historical mining landscapes, and the significant elevation changes that provide both a physical challenge and panoramic views of the surrounding Aragon region.

Q: Are there routes that offer panoramic views?
A: Absolutely. Many routes, especially those with significant climbs, offer panoramic views of the surrounding area. For example, the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e969135667" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Cucalón y Chevrons de Oriche - return from Huesa del Común&lt;/a&gt; route features substantial climbs and descents, providing ample opportunities for breathtaking vistas.

Q: Can I find routes that combine cycling with cultural exploration?
A: Yes, the region around Blesa is part of the 'Mining Trails' project in the Sierra de Arcos. These trails transform historical mining areas into an 'open-air museum' with informational panels, allowing you to combine your road cycling with cultural and historical exploration of the area's industrial heritage.

Q: Are there any routes suitable for families with children?
A: While many routes in Blesa feature significant elevation changes, families looking for a less strenuous ride might consider shorter, flatter sections of the moderate routes. Always check the route's elevation profile and distance to ensure it matches your family's capabilities. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1397378" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Santa Ana – Hills of La Hoz de la Vieja loop from Segura de los Baños&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 49.9 km route that could be an option for more active families.
tips: The municipality of Moneva is part of the Camp de Belchite region and is crossed by the Aguasvivas river, in an area with little vegetation and arid heaths. The municipality is also known for the Moneva reservoir, located to the north of the town.

Oliete is located above the riverbed of the Río Martín, where it is elevated from the rest of the land since the river has created a plain below the town, where there are currently agricultural fields.

Muniesa is a town that is located at the gates of the Iberian System, with small mountains around the town. As a curiosity, the term Muniesa comes from the Arabic MUNYA 'IṢA', the country house (with land) of Jesus.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the towns along the route, such as Moneva, Oliete, or Muniesa. As it's a circular route, you can start from any of these points.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the region's characteristics of sparse vegetation and dry heaths, spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant times to cycle. Summers can be very hot in this arid or semi-arid landscape, and winters might be chilly.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and paths.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is primarily a road cycling route. While dogs are generally allowed in public areas, it's not specifically designed for accompanying dogs. If you bring a dog, ensure it is well-behaved and kept on a leash, especially near traffic.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is mostly on well-paved surfaces, making it ideal for road bikes. You'll encounter rolling hills with a respectable amount of climbing, contributing to its moderate difficulty.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and points of interest can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: The route takes you through a landscape characterized by sparse vegetation and dry heaths, particularly around Moneva, where you might also see the Moneva Reservoir. Oliete is known for its Iberian culture, natural eccentricities, and the Sima de San Pedro, a large sinkhole. The route connects these settlements, offering a glimpse into the rural Aragon region.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate. With an elevation gain of approximately 880 meters over 67 kilometers, it requires a good level of fitness. Beginners might find the climbs challenging, so some cycling experience is recommended.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for this 67-kilometer route is around 3 hours and 9 minutes, but this can vary depending on your fitness level, speed, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: Are there cafes or places to eat along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through the towns of Moneva, Oliete, and Muniesa. You should be able to find cafes or restaurants in these settlements to refuel.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with several other paths, including sections of the Ruta Íbera, Por el río Martín, Sendero del Río Martín, and the Ferrocarril Zaragoza-Utrillas.

faqs.question: Is this route actually located in the Toledo steppe as the name suggests?
faqs.answer: Despite the name, this circular route is actually located in the province of Teruel, Aragon, rather than the Toledo steppe. The landscape, however, shares characteristics of dry heaths and sparse vegetation.
